narrative:
Rep. Gregory F. Murphy is a practicing urologist, the only surgeon in Congress who still sees patients, and a man who has parlayed his medical degree into a career of loudly criticizing the healthcare industry while quietly collecting its money [1]. Serving North Carolina's 3rd District since 2019, Murphy sits on the powerful Ways and Means Committee's Health Subcommittee -- a perch from which he has delivered some genuinely memorable lines, including calling health insurance companies less ethical than drug cartels [2] and suggesting UnitedHealthcare executives "ought to go to jail" [3].

To his credit, Murphy co-sponsored the TRUST in Congress Act to ban members from trading individual stocks [4], which aligns with Article 1 of the 80% Bill. He has also been a vocal advocate for breaking up vertically integrated insurance conglomerates and has pushed legislation to stabilize Medicare physician payments [5]. When it comes to healthcare transparency, Murphy talks a better game than most Republicans.

But here is where the stethoscope starts to pinch. Murphy's top industry donors include health professionals and pharmaceutical interests [6], the very entities regulated by the committees on which he sits. He co-chairs the House GOP Doctors Caucus, a group that has consistently opposed Medicare drug price negotiation expansions -- the kind envisioned in Article 7 of the 80% Bill [7]. His voting record on broader cost-of-living issues -- taxing the ultra-wealthy (Article 4), closing the carried interest loophole (Article 17), and banning corporate PACs (Article 5) -- has been a wall of "no" votes and conspicuous silences.

Murphy also voted against the Freedom to Vote Act and the John Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act (Article 8) [8], opposed paid family leave proposals (Article 14), and has shown no interest in Pentagon audit requirements (Article 6). On the DISCLOSE Act (Article 16) and dark money reform (Article 13), he has been reliably absent.

The pattern is familiar: a congressman who positions himself as a populist healthcare crusader while opposing the structural reforms -- campaign finance, tax fairness, voting rights -- that would actually change who gets to write the rules. Murphy's fury at insurance CEOs makes for great C-SPAN clips, but his voting record suggests the system that enriches his donors is one he is happy to leave intact [9]. North Carolina's 3rd District deserves a representative whose reform instincts extend beyond the hearing room.

Mary Miller represents Illinois's 15th Congressional District, a sprawling, deeply conservative swath of rural downstate Illinois that she has held since January 2021. A cattle farmer from Oakland, Illinois, Miller serves on the House Agriculture Committee and the House Education and the Workforce Committee [1]. She arrived in Washington with a memorable debut: on the day before the January 6 Capitol riot, she told a rally crowd that "Hitler was right on one thing — he said, 'Whoever has the youth has the future,'" a quote she later said was taken out of context, though audio recordings are notoriously resistant to context [2]. She quickly became one of the most reliably far-right members of the House, earning an "A" rating from the Heritage Action scorecard and a place in the House Freedom Caucus [3].

On the twenty issues that command supermajority support among Americans, Miller's record is a paradox of populist positioning and ideological rigidity. She has cosponsored congressional term limits legislation, a safe bet in deep-red districts where "drain the swamp" rhetoric never goes out of fashion [4]. She has backed efforts to protect U.S. farmland from foreign purchases, a natural fit for her Agriculture Committee seat and her own life as a farmer [5]. She supported the release of Epstein-related files [6]. But beyond those cost-free populist gestures, her record on the 80% Bill goes cold. She voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which contained the most significant Medicare drug price negotiation provisions in a generation [7]. She has never cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, ban corporate PACs, audit the Pentagon, enact paid family leave, or close the carried interest loophole. On campaign finance reform — banning dark money, passing the DISCLOSE Act — she is not merely silent but actively opposed, having voted against the For the People Act and similar democracy-reform packages [8].

The donor picture is revealing but uncomplicated. Miller's campaigns have been substantially self-funded, with her and her husband contributing significant personal wealth to her races [9]. Beyond family money, her top industry donors include agribusiness, oil and gas, and ideological conservative groups like the Club for Growth [10]. She has received support from corporate PACs in the agriculture and energy sectors — the same industries that benefit most from the tax loopholes and subsidies she has never moved to reform [11]. Her opposition to taxing the ultra-wealthy is perhaps less surprising when you consider that she and her husband own one of the larger farming operations in eastern Illinois.

Miller is, in many ways, a perfect case study in the gap between populist branding and legislative follow-through. She will fight to keep foreign companies from buying Illinois farmland, but she will not fight to keep corporate PACs from buying Illinois elections. She will call for term limits on other people's careers but has shown no interest in limiting the financial influence that makes those careers so lucrative in the first place. For the voters of IL-15 — many of whom support banning stock trading, negotiating drug prices, and ending dark money by margins well above eighty percent — the question is whether the cultural warfare is a sufficient substitute for the economic reforms they say they actually want.

Barry Moore is a Coffee County farm boy made congressman — an Auburn agricultural science grad, Army National Guard veteran, and industrial waste hauler turned career politician who has represented a slice of deep-red Alabama since 2021, first from AL-2 and then, after redistricting forced by the Supreme Court's ruling in Allen v. Milligan, from AL-1[1][2]. He's a proud member of the House Freedom Caucus, carries a Club for Growth seal of approval, and describes himself as a "Christian business owner" and defender of "sound money" — a characterization that has recently attracted $5 million in outside spending from Fairshake's crypto super PAC affiliate as Moore angles for Tommy Tuberville's open Senate seat[3][4].

On issues where the populist right and the 80% consensus converge, Moore is easy to find. He signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge before he was first elected and has cosponsored term limits resolutions in Congress[5]. He has backed legislation protecting American farmland from Chinese adversaries, citing his Agriculture Committee seat[6]. He voted for the Social Security Fairness Act in 2024[7] and for the Epstein Files Transparency Act, which passed 427-1[8]. He does not appear to hold individual stock in the companies over which he legislates — though he has not been a conspicuous champion of banning the practice for everyone else[9].

The harder reckoning comes when corporate money and populist principle share a ballot line. Moore voted against the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, which contained Medicare's first authority to negotiate prescription drug prices[10]. He opposed the Ukraine Security Supplemental in 2024, citing the national debt, yet has consistently voted to boost Pentagon appropriations despite the Department of Defense having failed eight consecutive audits[11][12]. In December 2023, he voted against the NDAA specifically because it "hiked spending by $9 billion despite the Pentagon recently failing its audit" — suggesting that when Pentagon waste intersects with culture-war objections, the culture war wins[13]. On January 6, 2021, Moore voted to object to the certification of Arizona's and Pennsylvania's electoral votes[14]. When the House voted 406-21 to award Congressional Gold Medals to the Capitol Police officers who held the line that day, Moore was among the 21 who voted no[15].

Follow the money and you find the usual architecture. Moore was bankrolled from the start by the Club for Growth[16]. His campaign disbursed over $38,000 to a storage business owned by his cousin at rates running nearly ten times the listed market rate, with some payments made to an address on land owned by Moore's parents[17]. Now a $5 million crypto super PAC has materialized around his Senate bid, representing an industry that has ranked him "strongly supportive" of crypto interests[3][18]. Barry Moore did not become a congressman by accident. He became one by being useful. The question Alabama voters face is useful to whom.

Blake Moore wants you to know he is very serious about fiscal responsibility. So serious, in fact, that in his first year in Congress he racked up 76 violations of the STOCK Act by failing to disclose stock trades worth between $70,000 and $1.1 million. The Campaign Legal Center filed an ethics complaint. The House Ethics Committee sprang into action and fined him a devastating $200 — roughly the price of one share of the stocks he forgot to mention. Business Insider gave him a "danger" rating for financial ethics. Moore called it an "oversight" and moved on to bigger things.

Those bigger things include his role as Vice Chair of the House Republican Conference and a coveted seat on the Ways and Means Committee, where he now helps write the tax code for the nation. From this perch, Moore has championed making the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act permanent — the law that slashed the corporate tax rate from 35% to 21% and, according to Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen, "disproportionately benefited the wealthy and large corporations." Moore disagreed with Yellen, naturally. He also runs the Expect More Leadership PAC, funneling donor money to fellow Republicans and cementing his status as a party insider.

Perhaps most revealing is Moore's proposal to put Social Security and Medicare funding up for annual congressional votes. He frames this as "accountability." Critics call it what it is: turning guaranteed benefits into a yearly political hostage negotiation. Moore wants Congress — the institution with a 20% approval rating — to have more power over whether seniors get their checks.

On the 80% Bill's reforms, Moore's record is a masterclass in selective populism. He has not co-sponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, despite personally demonstrating why such a ban is necessary. He has taken no position on the DISCLOSE Act, dark money bans, or corporate PAC restrictions. He fell asleep during the marathon Ways and Means markup of the "One Big, Beautiful Bill" — a $3.8 trillion tax cut package — which may be the most honest thing any congressman has done during a tax debate. On taxing the ultra-wealthy, closing loopholes, and auditing the Pentagon, Moore is silent. The man who couldn't be bothered to disclose his own stock trades would rather not discuss financial transparency for anyone else, either.

Frank Mrvan has represented Indiana's 1st congressional district since 2021, inheriting the seat from longtime congressman Pete Visclosky in a crowded fourteen-candidate Democratic primary that he won with just thirty-three percent of the vote [1]. The district covers northwest Indiana — Hammond, Gary, East Chicago, and the steel country that hugs the southern shore of Lake Michigan — and Mrvan is, in every way, a product of it. His father, Frank E. Mrvan, served in the Indiana state senate for decades, and the younger Mrvan spent sixteen years as North Township trustee before coming to Congress, a resume that practically screams machine politics [2]. Of Czechoslovak and Polish heritage, with a journalism degree from Ball State and prior careers in mortgage brokering and pharmaceutical sales, Mrvan is a blue-collar Democrat in the mold that the party keeps saying it needs more of — though whether the mold fits as snugly as advertised is worth examining.

On the eighty-percent agenda, Mrvan checks more boxes than most of his colleagues, though not as many as his labor-friendly branding might suggest. He is a cosponsor of the TRUST in Congress Act, the bipartisan bill to ban congressional stock trading by requiring members, spouses, and dependents to divest or use blind trusts [3]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included the most significant Medicare drug price negotiation provisions in decades, and spoke passionately on the House floor about seniors choosing between meals and prescriptions [4]. He cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act to combat dark money in elections [5]. He secured federal funding for police body camera programs in Gary, LaPorte, and Hobart — real money for real accountability in communities that need it [6]. And he publicly opposed mid-decade redistricting efforts in Indiana, calling them an attempt to silence voters [7]. On veterans' issues, he has been genuinely productive, shepherding legislation like the Dignity for MST Survivors Act and the VA Electronic Health Record Transparency Act into law during his time on the Veterans' Affairs Committee [8].

But the gaps are instructive. Mrvan has not championed congressional term limits — unsurprising for a man whose family has held public office in northwest Indiana for the better part of half a century. He has not led on banning corporate PACs, auditing the Pentagon, or closing the carried interest loophole. On issues like the right to repair, junk fees, and protecting farmland from foreign and corporate buyers, his public record is thin. For a Democrat representing a working-class district where these kitchen-table issues resonate deeply, the silence is notable — not damning, but notable.

The money tells a familiar Democratic story in a union district. Mrvan's endorsement list reads like a roll call of organized labor: the United Steelworkers, the IBEW, the Boilermakers, the Operating Engineers, the Iron Workers, and the Northwest Indiana Building and Construction Trades Council [9]. Labor PAC money is the lifeblood of his campaigns, and in return, Mrvan has been a reliable vote for steel tariffs, manufacturing protections, and worker-friendly legislation from his perch on the Appropriations Committee [10]. A 2022 Fox News report flagged roughly $4,000 in campaign expenditures on flights and seat upgrades for his wife, Jane — a small-bore controversy that his campaign defended as reflecting the "family commitment" of public service [11]. It is not a scandal that will bring anyone down, but it does suggest a certain comfort with the perks of office that sits awkwardly next to a populist brand.

Mrvan is, at bottom, a transactional Democrat — loyal to labor, attentive to veterans, and willing to cosponsor the right reform bills when the party asks. He is not a crusader for systemic change, and the dynasty politics of northwest Indiana have never demanded that he be one. His constituents in the steel towns and working-class suburbs of the Calumet Region deserve someone who fights as hard on dark money, Pentagon waste, and corporate PAC influence as he does on tariff policy. Whether Mrvan rises to that challenge or continues to play it safe will say a great deal about whether the Democratic Party's populist wing is real or just rhetorical.

Mariannette Miller-Meeks represents Iowa's 1st Congressional District, a southeastern Iowa seat she first won in 2020 by a margin of six votes — the closest House race in decades, certified only after a painstaking recount [1]. A retired Army ophthalmologist and former director of the Iowa Department of Public Health, Miller-Meeks brings legitimate medical credentials to Congress, where she serves on the Energy and Commerce Committee and the Veterans' Affairs Committee [2]. She likes to remind voters that she is a doctor, a veteran, and a first-generation American, a triple-barreled biography that would be more impressive if it translated into independence from the party apparatus that bankrolled her razor-thin victory.

On the twenty issues that command supermajority public support, Miller-Meeks occupies the familiar Republican position of talking populist while voting establishment. She has cosponsored congressional term limits legislation, a perennial crowd-pleaser that costs nothing because leadership never lets it reach the floor [3]. She has supported measures to protect U.S. farmland from foreign purchases, a defensible position in a state where Chinese and foreign corporate land acquisitions have become genuine front-page news [4]. She voted for the repeal of the 2002 Iraq AUMF, joining a bipartisan majority in a modest step toward reining in executive war powers [5]. And as a physician, she has spoken about lowering drug costs — though when the Inflation Reduction Act actually delivered Medicare drug price negotiation, she voted against it, calling it government overreach [6].

That vote captures the Miller-Meeks pattern: sympathetic rhetoric, opposing vote. She has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, end dark money, close the carried interest loophole, or mandate paid family leave. On auditing the Pentagon, a position supported by fiscal conservatives and progressives alike, she has been silent. She has shown no interest in banning corporate PACs or corporate purchases of single-family homes, and her record on police accountability measures like body camera mandates is nonexistent. The issues she does champion — term limits, farmland protections, Epstein file transparency — share a common trait: they are popular, symbolic, and utterly unthreatening to the donor class.

The donor class, meanwhile, has been generous. In her 2024 cycle, Miller-Meeks raised over $3 million, with significant contributions from health sector PACs, pharmaceutical companies, and insurance interests [7]. Her seat on the Energy and Commerce Committee, which oversees drug pricing, healthcare regulation, and telecommunications policy, makes her a high-value target for industry lobbyists [8]. The American Hospital Association, Blue Cross Blue Shield, and the American Medical Association have all been reliable contributors — the same organizations that spent millions opposing the Medicare drug negotiation provisions she voted against [9]. Real estate interests also feature prominently among her donors, offering useful context for her silence on banning corporate homebuyers [7].

Miller-Meeks is a congresswoman whose biography screams independence but whose legislative record whispers compliance. She served her country in uniform, healed people's eyesight for a living, and won her seat by the slimmest margin imaginable — and then arrived in Washington and voted like every other Republican whose campaign account is fattened by the industries she is supposed to oversee. In a district where Iowa farmers and small-town voters are desperate for someone who will actually challenge the system, Miller-Meeks offers the aesthetics of a maverick and the voting card of a loyalist.

Cory Mills is a Republican representing Florida's 7th Congressional District, covering the suburbs east of Orlando. A decorated Army veteran who served with the 82nd Airborne Division and Joint Special Operations Command, Mills earned a Bronze Star during a deployment to Iraq in 2003 [1]. After leaving the military, he co-founded PACEM Solutions International and PACEM Defense, private security and munitions companies that brokered a $228 million arms deal with Iraq facilitated by the Department of Defense [2]. He won his House seat in 2022 and promptly secured spots on the Armed Services and Foreign Affairs Committees — the two committees with the most direct oversight of the defense industry from which he built his fortune. The phrase "conflict of interest" does not begin to cover it.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Mills presents as a curious hybrid: a populist reformer on the issues that cost nothing and a reliable opponent on the ones that would. He has co-sponsored the bipartisan Restoring Faith in Government Act to ban congressional stock trading, joining forces with Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez and others in a rare cross-aisle moment [3]. He has signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge and co-sponsored H.J. Res. 12, a constitutional amendment to limit congressional terms [4]. He has voiced support for protecting American farmland from foreign adversaries [5]. These positions are popular, bipartisan, and — crucially — pose zero threat to his donor base. But on Medicare drug price negotiation, paid family leave, taxing the ultra-wealthy, banning dark money, closing the carried interest loophole, or the DISCLOSE Act, Mills has taken no meaningful action. His AFL-CIO lifetime score sits at 10%, and his Heritage Action score reached 98% in the 118th Congress [6], a voting record that leaves little room for the kind of structural reform the 80% Bill envisions.

Perhaps most revealing is Mills's approach to war powers. While the 80% Bill calls for repealing aging AUMFs and ending forever wars, Mills introduced a new narco-terrorism AUMF modeled on the notoriously broad 2001 authorization — no geographic restrictions, no named targets, just the president's discretion to deploy force against anyone he "determines" is a narco-terrorist [7]. For a man whose companies sell weapons to foreign governments, proposing new blank checks for military force is less a policy position than a business plan.

Follow the money and the contradictions sharpen. PACEM Defense and its affiliates racked up 94 federal contracts worth close to $1 million between January 2024 and 2025, while Mills sat on Armed Services [8]. The Office of Congressional Conduct found in August 2024 that these contracts represented "a clear and troubling conflict of interest," and in March 2025 the House Ethics Committee opened a formal investigation into whether Mills violated federal laws prohibiting members from contracting with the government [8]. Rep. Nancy Mace introduced a censure resolution calling for his removal from both committees, citing the defense contracts alongside allegations of campaign finance violations, undisclosed gifts, and sexual misconduct [9]. PACEM, meanwhile, owes $48 million to a Canadian lender and has been forced to close its munitions plant twice for failing to pay workers' compensation insurance [2].

Cory Mills will champion a stock trading ban and term limits — reforms that photograph well and threaten no one currently writing him checks. But the congressman who co-founded an arms company, sat on the committees overseeing that industry, and kept collecting federal contracts while in office is perhaps not the ideal messenger for draining any swamp. His record suggests that the 80% Bill's most uncomfortable provisions — auditing the Pentagon, ending forever wars, banning corporate PACs — are precisely the ones that would make his professional life considerably less profitable.

Jared Moskowitz is a Democrat representing Florida's 23rd Congressional District, which covers parts of Broward and Palm Beach counties in South Florida. A Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School graduate who went on to serve in the Florida legislature and then as the state's Director of Emergency Management under Ron DeSantis, Moskowitz arrived in Congress in 2023 with a resume that practically screamed bipartisan credibility [1]. He currently sits on the Foreign Affairs and Judiciary committees, serving as Ranking Member of the Foreign Affairs Subcommittee on Oversight and Intelligence [2]. He styles himself a centrist Democrat willing to cross the aisle — and he has, becoming the first Democrat to join the DOGE caucus in late 2024 [3], and voting for the Laken Riley Act alongside Republicans in 2025 [1]. The question, as always, is whether "bipartisan" means principled independence or strategic flexibility.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Moskowitz presents a genuinely complicated scorecard. He signed onto the bipartisan TRUST in Congress Act to ban congressional stock trading [4] — a noble gesture somewhat undermined by the fact that he has twice violated the STOCK Act by filing late disclosures on dozens of stock transactions [5]. In April 2025, he purchased between $20,000 and $300,000 in stocks across twenty companies while publicly lamenting market turmoil from Trump's tariff announcements; when the tariffs were paused days later, those stocks rebounded handsomely [5]. He has also raised alarms about DOGE accessing Treasury payment systems that handle Social Security and Medicare [6], which puts him on the right side of the "Fund Social Security" and government accountability questions — even if his membership in the DOGE caucus initially lent the operation a veneer of bipartisan legitimacy it arguably did not deserve. On the Pentagon audit front, his oversight role on Foreign Affairs gives him a platform, though he has not been a vocal champion of the cause.

Where Moskowitz genuinely diverges from the 80% Bill consensus is in the silences. He has not been a prominent voice on taxing the ultra-wealthy, banning corporate PACs, closing the carried interest loophole, or enacting paid family leave. On campaign finance reform — banning dark money, the DISCLOSE Act, overturning Citizens United — his record is thin for a Democrat, with no notable cosponsorship of the marquee bills. He has supported gun control measures and reproductive rights [1], but those are standard-issue positions for a South Florida Democrat rather than evidence of reformist zeal. On right to repair, junk fee bans, and preventing corporate purchases of single-family homes, his legislative footprint is minimal.

The money trail offers some context for these gaps. In the 2024 cycle, Moskowitz's top contributing industries included real estate, lawyers and law firms, and securities and investment firms [7]. For a congressman who has co-sponsored a stock trading ban while repeatedly running afoul of the existing disclosure rules, the prominence of Wall Street money in his fundraising is, at minimum, an irony worth noting. His 2022 Victory Fund drew contributions from entities like CDR Maguire Inc [8], and his overall fundraising profile leans heavily on the professional class that benefits most from the carried interest loophole and current campaign finance structures he has declined to challenge.

Moskowitz is, in the end, a more interesting case than the typical party-line vote. He has real credentials on emergency management and government oversight, and his willingness to criticize his own party earns him media attention and constituent goodwill. But the gap between his populist branding and his actual legislative priorities — particularly on stock trading transparency, campaign finance, and economic reform — suggests a congressman who knows exactly which popular positions to champion and which to quietly let slide. The 80% Bill asks whether representatives will side with the supermajority; Moskowitz's answer appears to be: selectively, and with an eye on his portfolio.

Rich McCormick brands himself as "Marine. Doctor. Outsider," and two of those three descriptors are accurate. A former Marine helicopter pilot who served over twenty years in the Corps and Navy with deployments to Afghanistan, Africa, and the Persian Gulf, McCormick earned his medical degree from Morehouse School of Medicine and completed his emergency medicine residency at Emory, training at Grady Hospital [1]. He worked as an ER doctor at Northside Hospital before winning Georgia's 6th District in 2022 and moving to the 7th after redistricting. He now sits on the Armed Services, Foreign Affairs, and Science, Space and Technology Committees [2]. The "outsider" label, however, has a limited shelf life for a man who has seamlessly adopted the insider habits of Washington, including some of the ones the 80% Bill is designed to end.

On the twenty provisions of the 80% Bill, McCormick's record is a masterclass in saying one thing and doing -- or not doing -- another. He has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, which is notable given that in September 2025, OpenSecrets reported he was two years late in disclosing two dozen individual stock transactions dating back to March 2023 [3]. Among the tardy disclosures were purchases of Apple, Cisco, Microsoft, L3Harris Technologies, and other companies whose industries fall directly under the jurisdiction of his Armed Services and Science committees. The man who ran as an outsider reformer apparently forgot the part where you tell the public what stocks you are buying while overseeing the industries that make them profitable. He has not cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act, has taken no public position on banning dark money or corporate PACs, and has shown no interest in a lifetime lobbying ban or congressional term limits -- the populist red meat that Republican primary voters devour but Republican incumbents quietly shelve.

On healthcare, the contradiction between McCormick the doctor and McCormick the congressman is striking. As an emergency physician, he saw firsthand what happens when patients cannot afford medication or delay care due to cost. As a congressman, he voted against the Inflation Reduction Act and its Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, siding with the pharmaceutical industry over the patients he once treated [4]. He has not championed right-to-repair legislation, paid family leave, or junk fee bans. On the Armed Services Committee, he votes reliably for increased defense spending without pushing for the Pentagon audit that the Department of Defense has now failed eight consecutive years running [5]. For a veteran who knows the military bureaucracy from the inside, his lack of interest in holding it accountable is conspicuous.

Follow the money and McCormick's positions begin to make more sense. FedEx Corp was the top contributor to Team McCormick in the 2024 cycle, providing $318,400, while his fundraising operation pulled in nearly $11 million through joint fundraising committees [6]. His top donor industries include defense contractors and health professionals -- the very sectors he oversees on his committees [7]. The late stock disclosures are not incidental; they are a window into the structural conflicts of interest that the 80% Bill's stock trading ban, corporate PAC ban, and DISCLOSE Act provisions are designed to eliminate. McCormick has received endorsements from Freedom Caucus-aligned figures, including the chairman of the Georgia Freedom Caucus, placing him firmly in the wing of the party that talks about draining the swamp while quietly restocking it [8].

The bottom line on Rich McCormick is that his resume is genuinely impressive -- combat veteran, emergency physician, small-business owner -- and his voting record is genuinely ordinary. He had the biography to be a credible voice for the 80% Bill's brand of bipartisan populism: audit the Pentagon he served in, ban the stock trading he violated disclosure rules on, negotiate the drug prices he watched patients struggle with. Instead, he chose the well-worn path of committee assignments that align with donor industries and late financial disclosures that suggest the rules are for other people. The outsider went inside and closed the door behind him.

James C. Moylan has served as Guam's non-voting delegate to Congress since January 2023, a Republican Army veteran who traded military service and a career as a parole officer for the Guam Department of Corrections for a seat in the House where he can speak but not cast a decisive vote [1][2]. Like his fellow territorial delegates, Moylan can vote in committee, introduce legislation, and offer amendments, but when the gavel falls on final passage, his vote disappears [3]. He sits on the Armed Services Committee, the Foreign Affairs Committee, and the Education and Workforce Committee [4], a portfolio that makes sense for a delegate representing an island where the U.S. military controls roughly a third of the land and where the ongoing Marine Corps buildup on Camp Blaz has reshaped the local economy [1].

On the 80% Bill's twenty priorities, Moylan's most notable contribution is the Best Price for Our Military Act, a bipartisan bill he introduced with Rep. Chris Deluzio (D-PA) in 2024 and reintroduced in 2025 [5][6]. The bill closes a loophole in the Truth in Negotiations Act that allows defense contractors to finalize deal terms before submitting certified cost and pricing data, a legalized form of the price gouging that has helped the Pentagon fail its audit for seven consecutive years [5][7]. The bill's language was included in the committee-passed version of the Fiscal Year 2026 National Defense Authorization Act [6]. It is the rare piece of legislation from a non-voting delegate that directly advances one of the 80% Bill's goals, in this case auditing the Pentagon and holding defense contractors accountable. On the remaining nineteen priorities, from banning congressional stock trading to ending dark money to term limits, Moylan's public record is largely silent.

Moylan's attendance record, while better than some territorial delegates, is still notable: from January 2023 to January 2026, he missed 201 of 589 roll call votes, a 34.1 percent absentee rate that far exceeds the 2.0 percent median for currently serving representatives [8]. Guam sits nearly 8,000 miles from Washington, and the travel burden on Pacific territory delegates is real, but missing a third of votes you already cannot cast on final passage compounds the democratic absurdity of territorial representation. His campaign finance profile is tracked by OpenSecrets [9], and his committee assignments on Armed Services place him squarely in the orbit of defense industry interests that dominate Guam's economy. Moylan has also championed veterans' causes, including an amendment to establish a permanent VA Regional Office on Guam [10] and a bill to cover shipping costs for adaptive vehicles provided to disabled veterans [11]. His record suggests a delegate focused on the military ecosystem that defines his territory, willing to challenge defense contractor waste in specific cases, but largely disengaged from the broader reform agenda that 80 percent of Americans say they want.
